---
schema: verdi.deviation/v1
covers: ef224c91e6994f61944b4ed05c5528dd5645962d
findings:
  - { id: judged-j-1, kind: judged, text: "RESTATEMENT (still present at HEAD): ac-1's 'carried over byte-for-byte' is implemented as a full frontmatter re-render (align.RenderMarkdown/renderFrontmatter over a value-copied struct), not a byte splice: on a strict-decode-legal living report whose frontmatter formatting differs from the canonical renderer (block-style findings list, alternate quoting), every OTHER finding's line and the digest/integrity/judge_integrity lines are silently rewritten into canonical form. The spec's two operational verifier checks (ComputeDigest equality, VerifyIntegrity success) still hold since they are value-level, and align-generated reports are always canonical, so this only diverges on hand-authored-but-legal reports — the letter of 'byte-for-byte' remains weaker than the implementation guarantees in that corner. Unchanged by ADJ-52/ADJ-53's fixes, which addressed the body-matching and exit-taxonomy findings only. (confidence 0.30)" }
  - { id: judged-j-6, kind: judged, text: "The verb's write is a plain in-place os.WriteFile (disposition.go:242) — truncate-then-write, no temp-then-rename — so an operational failure mid-write (disk full, kill, crash) leaves a TRUNCATED deviation-report.md: exactly the 'partial write' ac-2's refusal language forswears, landing on the one store file whose judged exchange 03 declares never reproducible, so content not yet committed to git (a just-recorded disposition, or the genuine judge_integrity record) is unrecoverable by any re-run. The repo's own shared primitive exists for this (internal/atomicfile, spec/shared-homes: 'the one shared atomic-write primitive for the store's mutable-zone files', fsync+rename) and sits unused here. cmd/verdi/align.go:214/251 shares the same raw-WriteFile shape, so the verb mirrors precedent rather than inventing the gap — but this spec is the one whose AC text promises 'never a silent or partial write', which currently holds only for the logic-level refusal paths, not the write itself. (confidence 0.20)" }
  - { id: judged-j-7, kind: judged, text: "runDisposition is an unlocked read-modify-write over the whole report (ReadFile at disposition.go:147 … WriteFile at :242 re-emits the entire file from the earlier decode) with no lock, no re-read, and no staleness check between the two: two concurrent invocations targeting DIFFERENT findings of the same report both exit 0 and print 'recorded', but the second write re-materializes the first finding's pre-race UNDISPOSITIONED state — a lost update in which a success claim ('recorded X -\u003e …') stands with no durable write behind it, contra ac-1's 'writes exactly one disposition' read under CLAUDE.md's never-fake-success rule, and contra ac-2's 'silent…write' framing (the clobber is silent). The same window lets the verb silently revert a concurrent `verdi align` regeneration. internal/filelock (I-12) guards only the serve/mcp writer and wtmanager lockfiles, and no other CLI verb locks either, so this is an unratified corner rather than a broken convention — but the spec's exactly-one-write language is what makes it visible on this verb. (confidence 0.15)" }
digest: sha256:78cf31ce6822c23bfcfda40017e3b1c7f68719e57167570066557e20a86a6bc4
integrity: sha256:a7de25b8d04621a070dcd356b13781bfbee0e4872cc24b263363c719fb3c41a2
judge_integrity: { stdin_b64: , raw_result: "```json\n{\"findings\":[{\"id\":\"j-1\",\"text\":\"RESTATEMENT (still present at HEAD): ac-1's 'carried over byte-for-byte' is implemented as a full frontmatter re-render (align.RenderMarkdown/renderFrontmatter over a value-copied struct), not a byte splice: on a strict-decode-legal living report whose frontmatter formatting differs from the canonical renderer (block-style findings list, alternate quoting), every OTHER finding's line and the digest/integrity/judge_integrity lines are silently rewritten into canonical form. The spec's two operational verifier checks (ComputeDigest equality, VerifyIntegrity success) still hold since they are value-level, and align-generated reports are always canonical, so this only diverges on hand-authored-but-legal reports — the letter of 'byte-for-byte' remains weaker than the implementation guarantees in that corner. Unchanged by ADJ-52/ADJ-53's fixes, which addressed the body-matching and exit-taxonomy findings only.\",\"confidence\":0.3},{\"id\":\"j-6\",\"text\":\"The verb's write is a plain in-place os.WriteFile (disposition.go:242) — truncate-then-write, no temp-then-rename — so an operational failure mid-write (disk full, kill, crash) leaves a TRUNCATED deviation-report.md: exactly the 'partial write' ac-2's refusal language forswears, landing on the one store file whose judged exchange 03 declares never reproducible, so content not yet committed to git (a just-recorded disposition, or the genuine judge_integrity record) is unrecoverable by any re-run. The repo's own shared primitive exists for this (internal/atomicfile, spec/shared-homes: 'the one shared atomic-write primitive for the store's mutable-zone files', fsync+rename) and sits unused here. cmd/verdi/align.go:214/251 shares the same raw-WriteFile shape, so the verb mirrors precedent rather than inventing the gap — but this spec is the one whose AC text promises 'never a silent or partial write', which currently holds only for the logic-level refusal paths, not the write itself.\",\"confidence\":0.2},{\"id\":\"j-7\",\"text\":\"runDisposition is an unlocked read-modify-write over the whole report (ReadFile at disposition.go:147 … WriteFile at :242 re-emits the entire file from the earlier decode) with no lock, no re-read, and no staleness check between the two: two concurrent invocations targeting DIFFERENT findings of the same report both exit 0 and print 'recorded', but the second write re-materializes the first finding's pre-race UNDISPOSITIONED state — a lost update in which a success claim ('recorded X -\u003e …') stands with no durable write behind it, contra ac-1's 'writes exactly one disposition' read under CLAUDE.md's never-fake-success rule, and contra ac-2's 'silent…write' framing (the clobber is silent). The same window lets the verb silently revert a concurrent `verdi align` regeneration. internal/filelock (I-12) guards only the serve/mcp writer and wtmanager lockfiles, and no other CLI verb locks either, so this is an unratified corner rather than a broken convention — but the spec's exactly-one-write language is what makes it visible on this verb.\",\"confidence\":0.15}]}\n```" }
provenance: { generator: verdi-align, version: v0, inputs: [spec/disposition-verb@ef224c91e6994f61944b4ed05c5528dd5645962d, spec/disposition-verb@d2ecf50f0e6f8a3163692abce22fe55de7adf3c2], digest: sha256:78cf31ce6822c23bfcfda40017e3b1c7f68719e57167570066557e20a86a6bc4, integrity: sha256:a7de25b8d04621a070dcd356b13781bfbee0e4872cc24b263363c719fb3c41a2 }
---
# Alignment report

## Computed

(none)

### Boundary diff vs acceptance baseline

(no impacted services)

### Diagram alignment

- (no accepted proposals)
- (no illustrative diagrams in this spec's body)

## Judged

- **judged-j-1** [UNDISPOSITIONED]: RESTATEMENT (still present at HEAD): ac-1's 'carried over byte-for-byte' is implemented as a full frontmatter re-render (align.RenderMarkdown/renderFrontmatter over a value-copied struct), not a byte splice: on a strict-decode-legal living report whose frontmatter formatting differs from the canonical renderer (block-style findings list, alternate quoting), every OTHER finding's line and the digest/integrity/judge_integrity lines are silently rewritten into canonical form. The spec's two operational verifier checks (ComputeDigest equality, VerifyIntegrity success) still hold since they are value-level, and align-generated reports are always canonical, so this only diverges on hand-authored-but-legal reports — the letter of 'byte-for-byte' remains weaker than the implementation guarantees in that corner. Unchanged by ADJ-52/ADJ-53's fixes, which addressed the body-matching and exit-taxonomy findings only. (confidence 0.30)
- **judged-j-6** [UNDISPOSITIONED]: The verb's write is a plain in-place os.WriteFile (disposition.go:242) — truncate-then-write, no temp-then-rename — so an operational failure mid-write (disk full, kill, crash) leaves a TRUNCATED deviation-report.md: exactly the 'partial write' ac-2's refusal language forswears, landing on the one store file whose judged exchange 03 declares never reproducible, so content not yet committed to git (a just-recorded disposition, or the genuine judge_integrity record) is unrecoverable by any re-run. The repo's own shared primitive exists for this (internal/atomicfile, spec/shared-homes: 'the one shared atomic-write primitive for the store's mutable-zone files', fsync+rename) and sits unused here. cmd/verdi/align.go:214/251 shares the same raw-WriteFile shape, so the verb mirrors precedent rather than inventing the gap — but this spec is the one whose AC text promises 'never a silent or partial write', which currently holds only for the logic-level refusal paths, not the write itself. (confidence 0.20)
- **judged-j-7** [UNDISPOSITIONED]: runDisposition is an unlocked read-modify-write over the whole report (ReadFile at disposition.go:147 … WriteFile at :242 re-emits the entire file from the earlier decode) with no lock, no re-read, and no staleness check between the two: two concurrent invocations targeting DIFFERENT findings of the same report both exit 0 and print 'recorded', but the second write re-materializes the first finding's pre-race UNDISPOSITIONED state — a lost update in which a success claim ('recorded X -> …') stands with no durable write behind it, contra ac-1's 'writes exactly one disposition' read under CLAUDE.md's never-fake-success rule, and contra ac-2's 'silent…write' framing (the clobber is silent). The same window lets the verb silently revert a concurrent `verdi align` regeneration. internal/filelock (I-12) guards only the serve/mcp writer and wtmanager lockfiles, and no other CLI verb locks either, so this is an unratified corner rather than a broken convention — but the spec's exactly-one-write language is what makes it visible on this verb. (confidence 0.15)
